docs.title: Sol-Engine
docs.text: Sol-Engine is an efficiency-oriented inference framework for high-resolution video diffusion. It wraps Cosmos3-Super, LTX-2.3, and SANA-Video with one explicit acceleration line per model.

docs.title: Models and speedups
docs.text: Model Params Acceleration line Speedup Cosmos3-Super (4xB200) 64B TeaCache + NVFP4 ~2.26x LTX-2.3 (1xB200) 22B cache + PISA + NVFP4 + token-prune .. 2.40x SANA-Video (1xB200) 2B EasyCache + fusion + compile 2.77x

docs.title: The five acceleration methods
docs.text:
Video diffusion inference exposes redundancy at three complementary levels: Algorithm level: adjacent denoising steps run structurally similar computation over slowly changing latents. Model level: long spatiotemporal sequences contain redundant tokens and attention interactions. Kernel level: DiT blocks repeatedly launch memory-bound work around GEMMs, layout movement, normalization, activation, and precision conversion.

Method Implemented entries Cache TeaCache, EasyCache, fixed-step cache Quantization NVFP4 Kernel fusion AdaLN gate fusion, GEMM epilogues, QKV merge, ... Sparse attention PISA, SpargeAttention, Sparse VideoGen, ... Token pruning Feature-norm pruning, ToMe-SD, Astraea, ...

docs.title: Quick start
docs.text:
In Claude Code or Codex, run:

/goal Execute the inference code for the three models using both baseline and full-opt
settings with the following requirements. Refer to AGENTS.md for the environment creation,
model download, and inference guides. For the environment, you need to create a new
environment. For model weights, you are allowed to reuse existing weights if they are
locally available; otherwise, you need to download them. Regarding adaptability, be aware
that the provided guides for environment creation, download scripts, and inference may
contain system incompatibilities, so you are expected to troubleshoot and adapt them to
your specific machine.

docs.title: Start here
docs.text:
• Installation: environment creation, CUDA JIT fixups, and model downloads.
  • Pipelines: optimized launch paths for Cosmos3-Super, LTX-2.3, and SANA-Video.
  • Techniques: the five acceleration methods and where they apply.

docs.title: Citation
docs.text:
@misc{li2026solvideoinferenceengine,
  title         = {Sol Video Inference Engine: Agent-Native Full-Stack Acceleration Framework for Efficient Video Generation},
  author        = {Yitong Li and Junsong Chen and Haopeng Li and Haozhe Liu and Jincheng Yu and Ligeng Zhu and Ping Luo and Song Han and Enze Xie},
  year          = {2026},
  eprint        = {2606.23743},
  archivePrefix = {arXiv},
  primaryClass  = {cs.CV},
  doi           = {10.48550/arXiv.2606.23743},
  url           = {https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.23743},
}

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• NVIDIA GPU and driver support for CUDA 13.
  • conda or miniforge, git, and a Hugging Face account/token.
  • Cosmos3-Super 64B uses 4xB200. LTX-2.3 and SANA-Video use 1xB200.

docs.title: Environment
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/NVlabs/Sol-Video-Inference-Engine.git Sol-Video-Inference-Engine
cd Sol-Video-Inference-Engine

PYTHON_VERSION=3.12 bash scripts/create_code_conda_env.sh
source "$PWD/scripts/use_code_storage_env.sh"
conda activate "$PWD/.conda/ltx23"

uv pip install -e "$PWD/python[diffusion]" --prerelease=allow

PYTHON_BIN=.conda/ltx23/bin/python bash scripts/postinstall_cuda_jit.sh

scripts/create_code_conda_env.sh creates .conda/ltx23 by default, installs pip and uv, and installs conda activation hooks for scripts/use_code_storage_env.sh. The script defaults to Python 3.11 when PYTHON_VERSION is unset; the agent guide sets PYTHON_VERSION=3.12 for this stack.

Add --with-te to scripts/postinstall_cuda_jit.sh when using the NVFP4 path for Cosmos3-Super or LTX-2.3.

docs.title: Verify
docs.text:
.conda/ltx23/bin/python -c "import torch, diffusers, sglang; print(torch.__version__, diffusers.__version__, torch.cuda.is_available())"

Expected versions are torch 2.11.0+cu130 and diffusers 0.38.0. torch.cuda.is_available() is false on CPU-only setup hosts.

docs.title: Model downloads
docs.text:
export HF_HOME="$PWD/.hf_cache"
huggingface-cli login

huggingface-cli download nvidia/Cosmos3-Super
huggingface-cli download Lightricks/LTX-2.3
huggingface-cli download Efficient-Large-Model/SANA-Video_2B_480p_diffusers

Convenience scripts are available under scripts/cosmos/, scripts/ltx/, and scripts/sana/ for resumable or Slurm-based downloads.

docs.title: Cosmos3-Super
docs.text: Cosmos3-Super is the 64B multi-GPU pipeline. The optimized configuration combines TeaCache with NVFP4.

docs.title: Speed
docs.text: Conservative full optimization uses TeaCache + NVFP4 and reaches ~2.26x speedup. Measured on 4x GB200 with 1280x720, 189 frames, and 35 denoising steps, warmup excluded.

docs.title: Launch
docs.text:
MODEL_REPO=nvidia/Cosmos3-Super \
ROOT=out/cosmos3 \
PROMPT_FILE=prompt.txt \
PROMPT_TAG=demo \
bash scripts/cosmos/slurm_cosmos3_super.sh baseline

MODEL_REPO=nvidia/Cosmos3-Super \
ROOT=out/cosmos3 \
PROMPT_FILE=prompt.txt \
PROMPT_TAG=demo \
bash scripts/cosmos/slurm_cosmos3_super.sh fullopt

The launcher contains Slurm headers for cluster use. On a single multi-GPU host, run it as a normal shell script after adjusting site-specific paths near the top.

docs.title: Techniques
docs.text:
• Cache: TeaCache uses residual similarity across denoising steps.
  • Quantization: NVFP4 is applied selectively, with first and last steps kept dense.

docs.title: LTX-2.3
docs.text: LTX-2.3 is the 22B high-resolution pipeline. The optimized path combines kernel fusion, cache, PISA sparse attention, NVFP4, and token pruning.

docs.title: Speed
docs.text: Full optimization uses kernel fusion + cache + PISA + NVFP4 + token-prune and reaches 2.40x speedup. Measured on GB200 with 1088x1920, 241 frames, warmup excluded.

docs.title: Launch
docs.text:
bash scripts/ltx/run_ltx23_sglang_hq_1080p10s.sh baseline
bash scripts/ltx/run_ltx23_sglang_hq_1080p10s.sh fullopt

fullopt is self-contained. Override MODEL_PATH, DISTILLED_LORA, or SPATIAL_UPSAMPLER when weights live outside the default cache location.

docs.title: Techniques
docs.text:
• Kernel fusion: kernel fusion reduces DiT operator overhead.
  • Cache: stage-specific reuse avoids redundant work.
  • Sparse attention: PISA targets redundant video self-attention.
  • Quantization: NVFP4 targets video FFN compute.
  • Token pruning: low-salience tokens are removed in selected refine steps.

docs.title: SANA-Video
docs.text: SANA-Video is the 2B text-to-video path in Sol-Engine. The optimized configuration uses EasyCache, attention/kernel fusion, and torch.compile.

docs.title: Speed
docs.text: Full optimization uses EasyCache + fusion + compile and reaches 2.77x speedup. Measured on GB200 with 832x480, 81 frames, and 50 denoising steps, warmup excluded.

docs.title: Launch
docs.text:
PY=.conda/ltx23/bin/python

$PY scripts/sana/sana_video_sglang_run.py \
  --model Efficient-Large-Model/SANA-Video_2B_480p_diffusers \
  --prompt "a corgi running on the beach" \
  --output out/sana_baseline

$PY scripts/sana/sana_video_sglang_run.py \
  --model Efficient-Large-Model/SANA-Video_2B_480p_diffusers \
  --prompt "a corgi running on the beach" \
  --output out/sana_fullopt \
  --easycache 0.1 --linattn-bf16 --qkv-merge --compile

docs.title: Techniques
docs.text:
• Cache: EasyCache reuses denoising work.
  • Kernel fusion: linear attention BF16 path and QKV merge reduce memory-bound overhead.

docs.title: Cache
docs.text:
Cache methods exploit algorithm-level redundancy across adjacent diffusion denoising steps. The latent state changes gradually, so selected intermediate work can be reused when a step is sufficiently similar to a prior step.

The paper frames cache as a cross-step family: the cache agent searches the policy, skip-step schedule, cached state, compensation rule, warmup, and maximum cached-step constraints for each deployment instance.

docs.title: In Sol-Engine
docs.text: Pipeline Cache path Role Cosmos3-Super TeaCache Residual replay across denoising steps LTX-2.3 Stage-specific cache Part of the full LTX optimization stack SANA-Video EasyCache Main SANA acceleration component

docs.title: Methods
docs.text: Method Role in the design space TeaCache timestep-conditioned residual replay, selected for Cosmos3-Super EasyCache runtime-adaptive feature reuse, selected for SANA-Video TaylorSeer feature forecasting with Taylor-series extrapolation Fixed-step cache deterministic fixed-step schedule, selected for LTX-2.3 Cache-DiT / DBCache block-level diffusion cache family used as a related cache baseline

docs.title: Practical notes
docs.text:
• Cache thresholds trade speed for visual fidelity.
  • Timing should be read from warmup-excluded log lines.
  • Quality checks should compare generated videos, not just total latency.

docs.title: Kernel fusion
docs.text:
Kernel fusion reduces memory-bound overhead inside DiT blocks. The target is the repeated glue around GEMMs: layout movement, normalization, activation, gate application, QK normalization, RoPE, and precision conversion.

The paper frames kernel fusion as a late-stage local-tuning problem: after other methods shift the bottlenecks, the fusion agent profiles fragmented operators and chooses custom kernels, compiler fusion, or GEMM epilogues for the current tensor shapes and precision formats.

docs.title: In Sol-Engine
docs.text: Pipeline Fusion path Role LTX-2.3 kernel fusion Operator-level optimization before other methods SANA-Video Linear attention BF16, QKV merge, compile Part of the 2.77x path

docs.title: Methods
docs.text: Method Role in the design space AdaLN and residual gate fusion AdaLN normalization, scale, shift, and residual gate fusion GEMM epilogues GEMM + activation / bias / FFN epilogue fusion described by CUTLASS and ByteTransformer-style paths QK-norm + RoPE fusion fused Q/K normalization and rotary-position work on attention Q/K paths Attention output gate fusion attention output projection, gate application, and output-path glue fusion Residual and modulation glue fusion residual update and modulation-path fusion around DiT blocks QKV merge merged projection path used in the SANA-Video optimization stack torch.compile compiler-driven graph fusion for stable operator sequences

docs.title: Scope
docs.text: Fusion should not change scheduler settings, prompts, seeds, or the number of denoising steps. It is an implementation-level optimization that reduces launch and memory traffic overhead.

docs.title: Quantization
docs.text:
Quantization targets kernel-level redundancy and bandwidth pressure by lowering precision where the model tolerates it.

The paper treats quantization as selective local tuning rather than uniform low-bit conversion. The quantization agent profiles layer sensitivity, tensor shapes, layer-wise precision choices, activation and weight bitwidths, and timestep-dependent scaling.

docs.title: In Sol-Engine
docs.text:
Pipeline Quantization path Notes Cosmos3-Super NVFP4 First and last denoising steps stay dense LTX-2.3 NVFP4 video FFN Used inside the full optimization stack

NVFP4 requires Blackwell GPUs and TransformerEngine. On older GPUs, the code falls back to BF16 with a warning.

docs.title: Methods
docs.text: Method Role in the design space NVFP4 production low-precision path used by Cosmos3-Super and LTX-2.3 Diffusion PTQ PTQ4DiT / Q-DiT / ViDiT-Q style diffusion-aware calibration family SVDQuant / Nunchaku outlier absorption with low-rank components for 4-bit diffusion inference SageAttention attention-specific 8-bit to 4-bit acceleration family ModelOpt / FP8 practical transformer checkpoint and runtime quantization family in the codebase

docs.title: Why boundary steps stay dense
docs.text: Early and late denoising steps are more sensitive to numerical error. Sol-Engine keeps those steps dense and applies NVFP4 where the speed/quality tradeoff is better.

docs.title: Sparse attention
docs.text:
Sparse attention targets model-level redundancy in long video token sequences. Many spatiotemporal attention interactions contribute little to the final output, so structured sparse patterns can reduce attention cost.

The paper lists sparse attention as a model-level family. The sparse-attention agent chooses the backend, sparsified layers, sparsity pattern, and compensation policy for the deployment instance.

docs.title: In Sol-Engine
docs.text:
LTX-2.3 uses PISA-style sparse video self-attention in selected stage-2 refinement work. It is combined with cache, fusion, NVFP4, and token pruning in the full optimization stack.

Implemented entries:

  • python/sglang/multimodal_gen/runtime/efficiency/transforms/sparse_attention.py
  • python/sglang/multimodal_gen/runtime/layers/attention/backends/piecewise_attn.py
  • python/sglang/multimodal_gen/runtime/layers/attention/backends/video_sparse_attn.py
  • python/sglang/multimodal_gen/runtime/layers/attention/backends/sparse_video_gen_2_attn.py
  • python/sglang/multimodal_gen/runtime/layers/attention/backends/sparse_linear_attn.py
  • python/sglang/multimodal_gen/runtime/layers/attention/backends/block_sparse_attn.py

docs.title: Methods
docs.text: Method Role in the design space PISA piecewise sparse attention selected for the LTX-2.3 full optimization stack SpargeAttention training-free block-wise sparse attention family Sparse VideoGen spatial-temporal sparse execution with online profiling Sparse VideoGen2 semantic-aware sparse-token identification and GPU layout VSA video sparse attention operator family

docs.title: Practical notes
docs.text:
• Sparse settings should be validated visually.
  • The value of sparse attention depends on sequence length and stage placement.
  • Report both denoise time and end-to-end time; decode and offload overhead can hide attention savings.

docs.title: Token pruning
docs.text:
Token pruning removes low-salience video tokens in selected steps. It targets model-level redundancy in long spatiotemporal sequences.

The paper treats token pruning as a sequence-reduction family: the token-pruning agent tunes the pruning criterion, pruning ratio, layer schedule, timestep schedule, and reconstruction rule, then validates temporal coherence and late-step fidelity.

docs.title: In Sol-Engine
docs.text:
LTX-2.3 uses token pruning in the full optimization stack together with kernel fusion, cache, PISA sparse attention, and NVFP4.

Implemented entries:

  • python/sglang/multimodal_gen/runtime/efficiency/techniques/token_prune.py
  • python/sglang/multimodal_gen/runtime/pipelines_core/stages/ltx_2_denoising.py
  • python/sglang/multimodal_gen/runtime/efficiency/models/ltx2_spec.py
  • python/sglang/multimodal_gen/runtime/efficiency/spec.py

docs.title: Methods
docs.text: Method Role in the design space Feature-norm pruning Sol-Engine LTX-2.3 midpoint token pruning implementation ToMe-SD diffusion token merging baseline family Astraea token-budget search for video DiTs TAPE temporally smoothed token importance for video diffusion CoReDiT spatially coherent pruning with reconstruction

docs.title: Practical notes
docs.text:
• Prune only in steps where visual sensitivity is acceptable.
  • Keep prompt, seed, scheduler, and resolution fixed when comparing variants.
  • Inspect videos side by side; scalar metrics are not enough for final acceptance.

docs.title: Cache-DiT / DBCache
docs.text: Cache-DiT is a diffusion-cache family that includes block-level reuse policies such as DBCache and feature forecasting variants. The paper discusses it as part of the broader cross-step cache landscape.

docs.title: Design point
docs.text: Method Skip scope Decision signal DBCache selected DiT blocks residual or feature difference TaylorSeer integration forecasted features Taylor-series extrapolation SCM-style masks denoising steps precomputed step mask

docs.title: Role in Sol-Engine
docs.text: Cache-DiT-style methods are useful baselines and candidates when a deployment benefits from block-level reuse instead of skipping an entire model path. They are not the selected default for the three reported full optimization stacks.

docs.title: Practical notes
docs.text:
• Block-level policies need careful quality validation.
  • A cache hit count is not the same as end-to-end speedup.
  • Decode, offload, LoRA switching, and stage transitions can hide denoising savings.

docs.title: EasyCache
docs.text: EasyCache is the cache policy selected for SANA-Video. It is a runtime-adaptive feature reuse method that balances speed and quality online instead of relying on a fixed offline schedule.

docs.title: Sol-Engine placement
docs.text:
Sol-Engine uses EasyCache in the SANA-Video denoising transformer path with --easycache 0.1 in the full optimization stack.

SANA-Video already uses an efficient linear-attention architecture, so EasyCache becomes the main algorithm-level acceleration component before kernel-level optimization is applied.

docs.title: Tunable knobs
docs.text:
• cache threshold: controls reuse aggressiveness.
  • warmup: keeps early denoising steps dense when needed.
  • subsampling: estimates feature change with reduced overhead.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text: Use the same prompt, seed, scheduler, resolution, and frame count when comparing baseline and full optimization outputs.

docs.title: Fixed-step cache
docs.text: Fixed-step cache uses a deterministic fixed-step schedule rather than a learned or threshold-driven skip decision. The paper reports that the LTX-2.3 high-quality schedule required a custom fixed-step strategy because cache schedules tuned for Euler-style samplers did not transfer cleanly to the res_2s trajectory.

docs.title: Sol-Engine placement
docs.text: Sol-Engine uses fixed-step cache in the LTX-2.3 stage-specific denoising path as the selected cache component in the full optimization stack.

docs.title: Why it is useful
docs.text: The LTX-2.3 pipeline has two stages and only a few high-resolution refinement steps. A deterministic schedule can be safer than threshold replay when the sampler trajectory is short, sensitive, or unlike the schedule assumed by generic cache heuristics.

docs.title: Tunable knobs
docs.text:
• skipped step indices.
  • stage placement.
  • recompute boundaries.
  • residual compensation rule.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text: Inspect stage-specific artifacts as well as final output. A schedule that helps stage 1 can still be unacceptable if it destabilizes stage 2 refinement.

docs.title: TaylorSeer
docs.text: TaylorSeer is a cross-step feature forecasting method. Instead of only replaying cached features, it extrapolates features from previous timesteps with a Taylor-series approximation to reduce error when reuse intervals become larger.

docs.title: Role in Sol-Engine
docs.text: TaylorSeer is part of the cache design space considered by the cache agent. It is useful as a candidate when plain residual replay produces too much drift at the desired skip interval.

docs.title: Search knobs
docs.text:
• forecast order.
  • warmup steps before forecasting.
  • maximum cached interval.
  • fallback recompute cadence.

docs.title: When to use
docs.text: Use TaylorSeer-style forecasting when the deployment needs more aggressive cross-step reuse than TeaCache or EasyCache can safely provide. Validate especially on motion consistency and high-frequency detail.

docs.title: TeaCache
docs.text: TeaCache is the cross-step cache policy selected for Cosmos3-Super. It estimates how much the denoising hidden state changes from one timestep to the next and reuses a cached residual when the accumulated change stays below a threshold.

docs.title: Sol-Engine placement
docs.text:
Sol-Engine uses TeaCache in the Cosmos3-Super generation-path transformer blocks with threshold 1.15, start step 10, and max 3 continuous hits.

The first cache hit skips repeated generation-path transformer work and replays the cached residual. A recompute refreshes the residual when the accumulated change crosses the threshold or the max-hit cap is reached.

docs.title: Tunable knobs
docs.text:
• threshold: lower is safer and slower; higher is faster and riskier.
  • start step: early steps are usually more structure-sensitive.
  • max continuous hits: caps drift from repeated replay.
  • cached state: residual replay is the default Sol-Engine path.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text: Compare full videos, not only scalar similarity. Cache methods can preserve layout while shifting fine detail, so visual validation remains the acceptance criterion.

docs.title: AdaLN and residual gate fusion
docs.text: AdaLN and residual gate fusion targets the normalization and modulation work that surrounds DiT block compute. The fused path keeps AdaLN normalization, scale, shift, and residual gate application close to the data they modify instead of materializing each intermediate through separate memory-bound operators.

docs.title: Role in Sol-Engine
docs.text: This is part of the LTX-2.3 kernel-fusion path. It is applied only where profiling shows the AdaLN and gate sequence is a meaningful overhead at the deployment shape.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text: The fused path should preserve the original block math within the expected BF16 rounding envelope. Compare outputs with the unfused path before combining it with cache, sparse attention, quantization, or token pruning.

docs.title: Attention output gate fusion
docs.text: Attention output gate fusion targets the path after attention has produced its output tensor. It combines output projection, gate application, and nearby layout or residual glue when the selected attention backend and tensor layout make that profitable.

docs.title: Role in Sol-Engine
docs.text: This fusion is useful when attention remains an active cost after sparse attention choices are fixed. It reduces launch overhead and avoids writing short-lived intermediates between the attention output and the block update.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text: Validate the fused path with the exact attention backend, precision format, and sequence shape used for deployment.

docs.title: GEMM epilogues
docs.text: GEMM epilogue fusion executes memory-bound follow-up work while intermediate GEMM results remain close to the compute kernel. The paper discusses CUTLASS epilogues, ByteTransformer-style fused bias / GELU paths, and CODA-style transformer block rewriting as representative kernel-fusion families.

docs.title: Typical fused sequences
docs.text:
• GEMM + bias.
  • GEMM + GELU.
  • FFN projection plus activation.
  • GEMM + residual update.
  • GEMM + normalization-related epilogue work.

docs.title: Role in Sol-Engine
docs.text: These fusions are candidates after profiling shows that fragmented memory-bound operators dominate the partially optimized stack.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text: Epilogue fusion must preserve numerics within the expected precision envelope and should be benchmarked at the actual deployment shape.

docs.title: QK-norm + RoPE fusion
docs.text: QK-norm + RoPE fusion combines Q/K normalization and rotary-position work on the attention Q/K path. This targets repeated small operators that otherwise launch separately and move intermediate tensors through memory.

docs.title: Role in Sol-Engine
docs.text: This is part of the DiT attention-path fusion design space and is especially relevant when attention remains active after sparse attention choices are fixed.

docs.title: Search knobs
docs.text:
• which attention blocks use fused Q/K preprocessing.
  • tensor layout expected by the downstream attention backend.
  • precision format used before attention.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text: Confirm compatibility with the selected attention backend and with any quantization path applied to QKV projections.

docs.title: QKV merge
docs.text: QKV merge combines separate query, key, and value projection work where the model and tensor layout allow it. In Sol-Engine, this is part of the SANA-Video full optimization path.

docs.title: Sol-Engine placement
docs.text: Sol-Engine uses QKV merge in the SANA-Video attention projection path together with EasyCache, BF16 linear-attention kernels, and compile.

docs.title: Why it helps
docs.text: Projection paths often launch multiple similar kernels and materialize intermediate tensors. Merging QKV work reduces launch overhead and improves data movement locality.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text: Keep the merged path equivalent to the original projections and verify output consistency before combining with EasyCache.

docs.title: Residual and modulation glue fusion
docs.text: Residual and modulation glue fusion targets the small operators that connect normalization, gates, residual updates, and block outputs. These operators are often individually cheap but collectively memory-bound when launched separately.

docs.title: Role in Sol-Engine
docs.text: This fusion is a local kernel-level optimization for DiT block glue. It is kept shape-specific and enabled only when profiling shows a real latency reduction.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text: Confirm that residual accumulation order, dtype conversion, and modulation values match the unfused path within the expected precision envelope.

docs.title: torch.compile
docs.text: torch.compile provides compiler-driven graph fusion and specialization for stable operator sequences. In Sol-Engine, it is used where compiler fusion is more practical than maintaining custom kernels for every shape.

docs.title: Sol-Engine placement
docs.text: Pipeline Scope Role SANA-Video compiled denoising path part of the 2.77x full optimization stack LTX-2.3 selected graph regions candidate when stable under target shapes

docs.title: Practical notes
docs.text:
• cold compile time should be excluded from warmup-excluded latency numbers.
  • compile mode and cache directory can change observed performance.
  • compiler fusion should be disabled for regions that deadlock or regress.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text: Run at least one warmup before quoting latency and compare generated video outputs under the same prompt and seed.

docs.title: Diffusion PTQ
docs.text: Diffusion post-training quantization methods study how to reduce precision while preserving denoising quality. The paper cites PTQ4DiT, Q-DiT, and ViDiT-Q as representative diffusion-aware calibration families.

docs.title: Design point
docs.text: Method family Main idea PTQ4DiT account for salient channels and timestep-varying activations Q-DiT allocate quantization granularity automatically for DiTs ViDiT-Q video diffusion quantization with diffusion-specific calibration

docs.title: Role in Sol-Engine
docs.text: These methods define the broader quantization search space. The reported Sol-Engine stacks use selective NVFP4 rather than a uniform PTQ conversion, but the same agent framework can evaluate diffusion PTQ candidates per deployment.

docs.title: Search knobs
docs.text:
• layer-wise precision.
  • activation and weight bitwidth.
  • timestep-dependent scaling.
  • ignored or fallback layers.

docs.title: ModelOpt / FP8
docs.text: ModelOpt and FP8 checkpoint flows are practical quantization families in the codebase. They are useful when a deployment has validated low-precision transformer components or when online quantization is acceptable for the target model.

docs.title: Role in Sol-Engine
docs.text: The reported full optimization stacks focus on selective NVFP4 for Cosmos3-Super and LTX-2.3, but ModelOpt / FP8 remains part of the broader local quantization search space.

docs.title: Practical knobs
docs.text:
• transformer override path.
  • transformer weights path.
  • ignored layer patterns.
  • layerwise offload compatibility.
  • BF16 fallback modules.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text: Quantized checkpoints should be validated in the same pipeline where they will be composed. A checkpoint that is acceptable alone may become unacceptable after cache or sparse attention is added.

docs.title: NVFP4
docs.text: NVFP4 is the production low-precision path used by the Sol-Engine full optimization stacks for Cosmos3-Super and LTX-2.3. The paper emphasizes that quantization is applied selectively, not uniformly.

docs.title: Sol-Engine placement
docs.text: Pipeline Scope Dense boundary Cosmos3-Super GEMM-heavy generation-path linear layers first 3 and last 3 denoising steps LTX-2.3 video FFN linears sensitive regions remain BF16

docs.title: Target modules
docs.text:
• FFN gate-up projection.
  • FFN down projection.
  • attention QKV projection.
  • attention output projection.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text: NVFP4 requires Blackwell-class hardware and TransformerEngine support. On unsupported hardware the runtime should fall back to BF16 instead of failing hard.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text: Boundary steps are kept dense because early steps affect global structure and late steps refine high-frequency detail. Any change to the dense boundary should be validated visually.

docs.title: SageAttention
docs.text: SageAttention is an attention-specific quantization family. The paper cites it as a method line that targets 8-bit to 4-bit attention acceleration with outlier smoothing.

docs.title: Design point
docs.text: Attention quantization differs from FFN quantization because attention scores and value aggregation can be sensitive to outliers and softmax behavior. SageAttention-style methods focus on this attention path rather than applying one uniform quantizer to the entire transformer.

docs.title: Role in Sol-Engine
docs.text: SageAttention is a candidate for deployments where attention remains a major bottleneck after cache, sparse attention, or token pruning choices are fixed.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text: Inspect both motion smoothness and temporal flicker. Attention quantization can preserve frame-level aesthetics while changing temporal consistency.

docs.title: SVDQuant / Nunchaku
docs.text: SVDQuant reduces low-bit quantization error by absorbing weight and activation outliers with low-rank components. Nunchaku is the practical inference family associated with SVDQuant-style 4-bit diffusion acceleration.

docs.title: Role in Sol-Engine
docs.text: SVDQuant is part of the quantization design space described by the paper. It is a candidate when a deployment needs 4-bit execution but direct low-bit conversion causes outlier-driven quality loss.

docs.title: Practical path
docs.text:
• keep base model metadata separate from quantized transformer weights when possible.
  • use explicit transformer override paths for quantized components.
  • validate fallback layers and outlier handling before combining with cache or sparse attention.

docs.title: Trade-off
docs.text: SVDQuant can preserve quality better than naive 4-bit quantization, but it adds method-specific checkpoint and runtime requirements.

docs.title: PISA
docs.text: PISA is the sparse attention method selected for LTX-2.3. It uses piecewise sparse attention: important blocks are computed exactly, while less important blocks can be approximated or skipped depending on configuration.

docs.title: Sol-Engine placement
docs.text: Sol-Engine uses PISA in selected LTX-2.3 stage-2 video self-attention blocks together with cache, token pruning, NVFP4, and kernel fusion.

docs.title: Tunable knobs
docs.text:
• sparsity.
  • block size.
  • route mode.
  • dense fallback layers.
  • approximate-remainder policy.
  • stage and layer placement.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text: Validate temporal coherence and fine detail. Sparse attention errors can be subtle in single frames but visible over motion.

docs.title: SpargeAttention
docs.text: SpargeAttention is a training-free sparse attention family cited by the paper. It predicts near-zero attention entries with a block-wise similarity metric and skips low-value work.

docs.title: Role in Sol-Engine
docs.text: SpargeAttention is part of the sparse-attention design space. It is a candidate when the attention map has clear block-level structure and the deployment can tolerate approximate attention entries.

docs.title: Search knobs
docs.text:
• block size.
  • similarity threshold.
  • dense fallback layers.
  • per-stage sparsity budget.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text: Compare against dense attention at the target resolution and duration; sparsity that works at shorter context can fail at longer temporal length.

docs.title: Sparse VideoGen
docs.text: Sparse VideoGen exploits spatial-temporal sparsity patterns in video diffusion with online profiling and sparse execution.

docs.title: Role in Sol-Engine
docs.text: It is part of the sparse-attention method family considered by the sparse-attention agent. It is most relevant when profiling shows persistent spatial-temporal sparsity across the target prompts and generation length.

docs.title: Practical knobs
docs.text:
• profiling window.
  • selected sparse pattern.
  • custom sparse-kernel layout.
  • dense fallback schedule.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text: Validate at the exact video duration and resolution. Sparse patterns can be configuration-specific.

docs.title: Sparse VideoGen2
docs.text: Sparse VideoGen2 improves sparse-token identification and GPU layout through semantic-aware permutation.

docs.title: Role in Sol-Engine
docs.text: It is a candidate sparse backend when semantic grouping can make sparse execution more efficient or more stable than a purely geometric block pattern.

docs.title: Search knobs
docs.text:
• semantic grouping policy.
  • sparse-token identification threshold.
  • layout permutation.
  • dense fallback layers.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text: Inspect whether semantic grouping preserves object boundaries and motion continuity across frames.

docs.title: VSA
docs.text: VSA is a video sparse attention operator family cited by the paper. It targets redundant attention interactions in video token sequences.

docs.title: Role in Sol-Engine
docs.text: VSA is part of the sparse-attention candidate set. It may be useful when the target model exposes stable video-specific sparse attention patterns.

docs.title: Practical knobs
docs.text:
• operator backend.
  • sparsity pattern.
  • stage placement.
  • dense fallback layers.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text: Use side-by-side videos and report both denoise time and end-to-end time. Attention savings can be hidden by decode or offload overhead.

docs.title: Astraea
docs.text: Astraea searches token budgets for video DiTs under a performance target. It treats token reduction as a budget allocation problem rather than a fixed pruning rule.

docs.title: Role in Sol-Engine
docs.text: Astraea is part of the token-pruning method family cited by the paper. It is useful when the target deployment needs an explicit speed budget and can search pruning ratios across layers or timesteps.

docs.title: Search knobs
docs.text:
• token budget.
  • layer schedule.
  • timestep schedule.
  • quality constraint.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text: Use a fixed validation set and inspect representative videos before composing the candidate with sparse attention or cache.

docs.title: CoReDiT
docs.text: CoReDiT prunes spatially coherent DiT tokens and reconstructs skipped outputs to preserve a dense representation for later layers or stages.

docs.title: Role in Sol-Engine
docs.text: CoReDiT is part of the token-pruning family cited by the paper. It is relevant when spatial coherence matters more than raw token-count reduction.

docs.title: Search knobs
docs.text:
• coherent region selection.
  • pruning ratio.
  • reconstruction rule.
  • layer and timestep placement.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text: Inspect object boundaries, small structures, and temporal motion. Reconstruction should not create persistent spatial artifacts.

docs.title: Feature-norm pruning
docs.text: Feature-norm pruning is the Sol-Engine token-pruning implementation used for LTX-2.3. It scores video tokens by feature norm, keeps the highest-salience tokens, runs the expensive block stack on that subset, and compensates dropped tokens from prior state.

docs.title: Sol-Engine placement
docs.text: Sol-Engine uses feature-norm pruning in LTX-2.3 stage-2 midpoint steps together with PISA, kernel fusion, cache, and NVFP4.

docs.title: Tunable knobs
docs.text:
• keep ratio.
  • pruning steps.
  • salience metric.
  • compensation rule.
  • prunable token seam.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text: Middle-step pruning should preserve temporal coherence and late-step detail. Do not accept it from latency alone.

docs.title: TAPE
docs.text: TAPE smooths token importance across frames to reduce temporal jitter in video diffusion token reduction.

docs.title: Role in Sol-Engine
docs.text: TAPE is part of the token-pruning design space for long videos, where per-frame independent pruning decisions can create flicker.

docs.title: Search knobs
docs.text:
• importance smoothing window.
  • keep ratio.
  • layer placement.
  • timestep placement.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text: Focus on temporal flicker, subject consistency, and motion smoothness. A method can look acceptable frame-by-frame but fail over time.

docs.title: ToMe-SD
docs.text: ToMe-SD is a token merging method for diffusion inference. The paper cites it as an early example showing that redundant diffusion tokens can be merged during inference with limited quality loss.

docs.title: Role in Sol-Engine
docs.text: ToMe-SD is part of the token-reduction design space. It is a candidate when merging similar tokens is safer than dropping tokens outright.

docs.title: Search knobs
docs.text:
• merge ratio.
  • layer placement.
  • timestep schedule.
  • similarity metric.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text: Token merging can blur fine detail. Inspect texture, object boundaries, and motion consistency.
